relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Balaam stops hunting for enchantment and turns toward the wilderness.
He sees Israel ordered by tribes, and spirit rests upon the foreign seer.
The oracle begins from an eye being opened.
He hears El and falls beneath a vision with uncovered sight.
How good are your tents, Jacob, your dwelling places, Israel.
The people appear like watered valleys, gardens, aloes, and cedars.
The blessing overflows into water, descendants, and royal strength.
Exodus liberation becomes the oracle's image of power.
Israel rests like a lion, and blessing returns upon those who bless.
Balak's anger erupts because three curses have become three blessings.
The king dismisses Balaam and calls obedience to YHWH lost honor.
Balaam reminds him that this boundary was stated from the beginning.
No house of silver and gold can purchase speech from his own heart.
Before leaving, Balaam turns toward a distant political horizon.
The seer again names the opened eye.
The oracle claims hearing, knowledge, vision, and surrendered sight.
A distant star and scepter rise from Jacob in ancient royal hope.
The vision moves into conquest over Edom and Seir.
Rule from Jacob is pictured through the destruction of resistance.
Amalek's beginning and end are compressed into an oracle of ruin.
The Kenite nest appears secure in rock.
Even that security falls under Assyrian captivity.
The seer laments the human cost of imperial upheaval.
Kittim, Asshur, and Eber pass through another cycle of affliction and collapse.
Balaam and Balak separate, the purchased curse never delivered.

contemplative_note: Numbers 24 begins with beauty—tents, gardens, water, blessing—and ends in compressed visions of empire and ruin. The mystical layer receives the beauty without baptizing domination: no star, scepter, or oracle is permission for modern conquest.
